coredata.dc:title: Interfacial polymerization nanofiltration membrane with visible light photocatalytic self-cleaning performance by incorporation of CQD/TiO2
coredata.dc:creator: Mi, Yifang
Wang, Na
Fang, Xingyu
Cao, Jia
Tao, Meng
Cao, Zhihai
coredata.dc:description: Integration photocatalysis with membrane technology offers a potential solution to combat fouling dilemma. Herein, an interfacial polymerization polyamide nanofiltration (NF) membrane with self-cleaning ability was prepared by incorporation of carbon quantum dots /titanium dioxide (CQD/TiO2) nanoparticles (NPs). The addition of hydrophilic CQD/TiO2 NPs promoted membrane hydrophilicity and endowed the NF membrane with high water flux (67.22 L m−2h−1), which was 1.8 times compared with that of the pristine membrane, and salt rejection of Na2SO4 (96.7%). Simultaneously, the prepared NF membranes maintained high dye rejection to methyl blue (MYB, 99.1%) and congo red (99.8%). Moreover, visible light-driven photocatalytic ability of CQD/TiO2 allowed the membranes to efficiently degrade MYB dyes absorbed on the membrane surface. In stark contrast to the results of cyclic physical water cleaning, the flux recovery ratios were maintained 97–98% without sacrificing MYB rejections during the cyclic fouling-irradiation process, demonstrating stable and efficient self-cleaning performance. Even after continuous membrane fouling for 72 h, the water flux only decreased by 6.7% after visible light irradiation, surpassing the performance of pristine polyamide NF membrane (decreased by 23.4%). The hydrophilicity and visible light-driven self-cleaning ability make the prepared NF membrane via interfacial polymerization highly suitable for efficient dye wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofiltration membranes
Interfacial polymerization
Carbon quantum dots
Photocatalytic
Self-cleaning

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of photocatalytic self-cleaning NF membranes
NF performance of photocatalytic self-cleaning NF membranes
Preparation and characterizations of CQD/TiO2 NPs
Fabrication and characterizations of photocatalytic self-cleaning NF membranes
NF performance of photocatalytic self-cleaning membranes
Antifouling performance of CQD/TiO2–mediated NF membrane
Self-cleaning and stability study of CQD/TiO2–mediated NF membrane

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21908200 and 51573168 ), the Talent Scientific Research Fund of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University (No. 18012218-Y ), the Key Research & Development Program of Zhejiang Province (2021C03196), and National Undergraduate Training Programs for Innovation and Entrepreneurship of China (201910338041).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of (a) CQD/TiO2 NPs and (b) photocatalytic self-cleaning NF membranes via interfacial polymerization.
(a) FTIR spectra of TiO2, CQDs, and CQD/TiO2 NPs; (b) XRD patterns of CQDs, TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 NPs; (c) TEM image and (d) HRTEM image of CQD/TiO2 NPs.
(a) UV–vis spectra of CQDs, TiO2, and CQD/TiO2 NPs; (b) Degradation of MYB (20 ppm) by TiO2 and CQD/TiO2 NPs under visible light irradiation for 1 h.
(a) EDS mapping of Ti element of PA-4 and (b) Water dispersions of TiO2 NPs and CQD/TiO2 NPs.
Cross-section morphologies and AFM images of PA-1 and PA-4.
(a) The effect of CQD/TiO2 NPs concentrations on NF performance and (b) water contact angles of the corresponding NF membranes.
The rejections of PA-4 for various inorganic salts and dyes (a); Zeta potential of PA-1 and PA-4 (b); (c) PEG rejections and (d) stability test of PA-1 and PA-4.
Antifouling performances of PA-1 and PA-4 tested with 0.1 g L-1 MYB (a) and BSA (b) aqueous solution, respectively.
NF performance of PA-4 after cyclic DI water washing and visible light irradiation (25 °C, 0.6 MPa, 100 ppm MYB solution).
(a) Photography of the fouled PA-1 and PA-4 membrane before and after visible light irradiation for 1 h; NF performance of PA-1 (b) and PA-4 (c) before and after visible light irradiation.
Surface chemical compositions of PA-1 and self-cleaning membrane PA-4 by XPS.

head.ce:title: Interfacial polymerization nanofiltration membrane with visible light photocatalytic self-cleaning performance by incorporation of CQD/TiO2

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Advanced Textile Materials and Manufacturing Technology and Engineering Research Center for Eco-Dyeing & Finishing of Textiles, Ministry of Education, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Textile Materials and Manufacturing Technology and Engineering Research Center for Eco-Dyeing & Finishing of Textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Advanced Textile Materials and Manufacturing Technology and Engineering Research Center for Eco-Dyeing & Finishing of Textiles, Ministry of Education, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Integration photocatalysis with membrane technology offers a potential solution to combat fouling dilemma. Herein, an interfacial polymerization polyamide nanofiltration (NF) membrane with self-cleaning ability was prepared by incorporation of carbon quantum dots /titanium dioxide (CQD/TiO2) nanoparticles (NPs). The addition of hydrophilic CQD/TiO2 NPs promoted membrane hydrophilicity and endowed the NF membrane with high water flux (67.22 L m−2h−1), which was 1.8 times compared with that of the pristine membrane, and salt rejection of Na2SO4 (96.7%). Simultaneously, the prepared NF membranes maintained high dye rejection to methyl blue (MYB, 99.1%) and congo red (99.8%). Moreover, visible light-driven photocatalytic ability of CQD/TiO2 allowed the membranes to efficiently degrade MYB dyes absorbed on the membrane surface. In stark contrast to the results of cyclic physical water cleaning, the flux recovery ratios were maintained 97–98% without sacrificing MYB rejections during the cyclic fouling-irradiation process, demonstrating stable and efficient self-cleaning performance. Even after continuous membrane fouling for 72 h, the water flux only decreased by 6.7% after visible light irradiation, surpassing the performance of pristine polyamide NF membrane (decreased by 23.4%). The hydrophilicity and visible light-driven self-cleaning ability make the prepared NF membrane via interfacial polymerization highly suitable for efficient dye wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: The applications of membrane separation technologies have emerged as an essential way to address the global water crisis [1]. Thereinto, nanofiltration (NF) membranes, which pore sizes intermediate between ultrafiltration and reverse osmosis membranes, outperform other membranes in realizing separation of small organic molecular (200 to 1000 Da) and inorganic salts, relying on the Donnan and size exclusion mechanism [2,3]. The merits of NF membranes (high separation efficacy, low energy consumption, etc.) enable their wide fit-for-purpose applications, such as water purification, pharmaceuticals, food industry, and so forth.

However, NF membrane fouling is still an obstacle that must be solved because it deteriorates separation performance and shortens lifespan of membranes [4]. This issue is more serious when dealing with textile wastewater. For these reasons, alleviating fouling with effective strategies is still a hotspot in membrane technology research. Massive strategies are focusing on passive approaches designed to decrease the fouling rate, normally by creating a hydration layer with hydrophilic surface modification or incorporation of hydrophilic inorganic nanoparticles (NPs) [5–10]. However, these modified NF membranes are still inevitably succumbed to foulants absorption or deposition during continuous operation, thereby impairing the competitiveness and efficiency of NF membrane technology.

Photocatalysis is one effective and green strategy to eliminate fouling problems, endowing NF membrane with self-cleaning ability [11]. An electron of photocatalyst is excited from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) by absorption of light, resulting in a positive charge remaining in the VB and photocatalytic reactions initiate [9]. The formation of an electron-hole pair further drives the decomposition of organic matter. Recently, NPs with photocatalytic functions such as graphitic nitrogen carbide, zinc oxide, metal − organic framework, graphene oxide, and so forth, have been successfully implemented in the preparation of membranes [12–14]. Among these photocatalysts, TiO2 is the most employed photocatalyst due to its good hydrophilicity, high stability, high activity, low cost, and low toxicity [15]. Particularly, TiO2 can facilitate membrane permeability and concurrently realize self-cleaning by photocatalytic-degrading organic pollutants [11]. Albeit with many successes, the practical applications of TiO2 are challenged by some inherent properties. The ultraviolet (UV) irradiation is necessary to excite electrons due to the wide bandgap energy of anatase phase TiO2. Solar utilization efficiency was restricted because of the low proportion of UV light in sunlight and low photocatalytic efficiency resulted from the quick recombination of electrons and holes [16–18]. Often, irreversible membrane damages are associated with the utilization of UV irradiation. These challenges restrict the development of TiO2 photocatalytic separation membranes in water treatments. Developing visible light-driven TiO2-based catalysts with low cost have been considered as a promising way to ameliorate obstacles of TiO2.

Doped TiO2 has been considered as an encouraging strategy to enhance visible light absorption [19–21]. Nanocarbon is one of the most efficient dopants to promote charge separation/transfer/utilization and significantly improve the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 [22]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new generation of nanocarbon, not only possess nice light absorptivity and carrier conductivity but also show bulky molecular features, which can easily be doped into TiO2 [23,24]. And the resultant heavy-metal-free CQDs/TiO2 composites NPs (CQD/TiO2 NPs) have been used as effective visible light-driven catalysts for organic compounds degradation [25–27]. Furthermore, nontoxic CQDs and TiO2 are easily available, and CQDs can be easily prepared from many kinds of natural biomass or common experimental reagents, providing practicable favor to construct CQD/TiO2 NPs.

Normally, integrations of photocatalytic NPs into NF membranes are mainly through phase inversion [19–21] and filtration/surface deposition [28–30] methods. For example, Heng et. al developed a self-cleaning polyethersulfone membrane by deposition of nitrogen-doped CQD modified TiO2, however, UV-induced graft polymerization of polyacrylic acid on polyethersulfone was needed before immobilization of NPs [31]. Most commercial NF membranes are polyamide NF membranes fabricated via interfacial polymerization [32]. In a typical process, amino monomers dissolved in water diffuse to the organic phase where they react with acyl chloride at the water/organic interface to generate polyamide NF membranes. Due to its remarkable efficiency and robustness, interfacial polymerization has been evolved as a powerful method for preparing separation membranes in water desalination. Thus, integrations of photocatalytic NPs with interfacial polymerization method have a significance of practical applications. The applications of photocatalytic CQD/TiO2 NPs in interfacial polymerization NF membrane have not been fully explored. Given this, the exploration of CQD/TiO2 NPs in interfacial polymerization was conducted. The elaborately synthesized and characterized CQD/TiO2 NPs were incorporated in aqueous solutions in interfacial polymerization, then, a series of photocatalytic self-cleaning NF membranes were fabricated. The obtained visible light-driven photocatalytic membranes with self-cleaning performance were systematically evaluated in terms of water fluxes, solute rejections, as well as photocatalytic degradations, to substantiate the significant influence of CQD/TiO2 NPs. The integration of membrane separation and visible light-driven photocatalytic degradation ability offers a possibility for longer continuous membrane operation.

Citric Acid (CA), piperazine (PIP), trimesoyl chloride (TMC), dopamine, tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane(Tris), methyl blue (MYB), congo red (CR), methylene blue (MB), victoria blue B (VBB), polyethylene glycol (PEG, 200, 400, 600, 800, and 1000 Da), and bovine serum albumin(BSA) were received from Shanghai Aladdin Co., Ltd. TiO2 with an average particle size of 21 nm was purchased from Evonik Degussa Shanghai Co., Ltd. Hexane, magnesium chloride (MgCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory. All chemicals were used as received Polysulfone supporting membranes (PSF, 20 kDa) were supplied by Hangzhou Development Center of Water Treatment Technology. Deionized (DI) water was adopted in all experiments.

CQDs were obtained by direct pyrolyzing CA as reported in previous work [33]. Specifically, 2 g CA was pyrolyzed at 200 °C for 30 min producing an orange-brown viscosity liquid. The liquid was dissolved in DI water followed by neutralization to pH 7 with 1 M aqueous NaOH solution. The crude product was filtered with 0.22 μm filter membranes to remove large particles and then further purified through dialysis using 1 K MWCO tube to remove residual CA, followed by freeze-drying for 24 h to obtain solid CQDs. CQD/TiO2 NPs were prepared by a hydrothermal method as revealed in Fig. 1 a [34]. Briefly, 0.1 g TiO2 was dispersed in 15 mL DI water and sonicated for 15 min, followed by the addition of 15 mg CQD (1 g L−1). The mixture was stirred for 2 h and sonicated for 30 min. Then the homogeneous dispersion was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 130 °C for 2 h. Finally, the resultant CQD/TiO2 NPs were centrifuged (5000 rpm for 15 min), washed with DI water for purification. The solid CQD/TiO2 NPs were acquired by lyophilization of the purified samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of photocatalytic self-cleaning NF membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The typical interfacial polymerization was performed to fabricate photocatalytic self-cleaning NF membranes as illustrated in Fig. 1b. To improving the stability of NF membrane, polydopamine was utilized to modify the surface of PSF membrane first [35–37]. Specifically, 1 g L−1 dopamine was dissolved in Tris-HCl solution (pH 8.5) for 1 h, then the PSF surface was impregnated with dopamine solution for 30 min. The unreacted dopamine was washed away with DI water. CQD/TiO2 NPs were dispersed uniformly in 0.35 wt% PIP aqueous solution (pH = 12) with the aid of sonication. The mixture was poured on the surface of polydopamine-coated PSF membrane for 1 min. After draining the excess aqueous solution, the obtained membrane was air-dried at room temperature until no liquid droplets on it. Thereafter, the NF selective separation layer was generated by immersing the membrane surface in 0.1 wt% TMC/hexane solution for 30 s. Finally, the resultant photocatalytic self-cleaning NF membrane was thermally treated at 50 °C for 15 min after the removal of TMC solution. According to the contents of CQD/TiO2 NPs (0、0.010 wt%、0.0175 wt%、0.035 wt%, and 0.07 wt%), the photocatalytic self-cleaning NF membranes were named as PA-(1–5).

Morphologies of CQDs and CQD/TiO2 NPs were visualized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100) with an operating voltage of 200 kV. The crystal structures of CQDs and CQD/TiO2 NPs were recorded by a Powder X-rays diffractometer (XRD, BRUKER AXS) with a scanning range from 10° to 80°. The chemical structures were characterized by Vertex Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR). UV–vis spectra were characterized by UV–vis spectrometer (UV-2600).

The chemical structures and element compositions of PA-(1–5) were characterized by attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Nicolet Aratar 370) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific), respectively. The morphologies of membranes were characterized by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, GeminiSEM500) equipped with an EDS. Atomic force microscopy (AFM, XE-100E) was applied to obtain the membrane roughness. The water contact angles of NF membranes were assessed by Kruess drop contract-angle system in sessile drop mode at ambient temperature. Membrane surface potentials at various pH were studied by SurPASS Anton Paar Zeta potential analyzer using 1 mM KCl as background solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NF performance of photocatalytic self-cleaning NF membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The NF performances of photocatalytic self-cleaning NF membranes were measured by a cross-flow filtration instrument at 25 °C, 0.6 MPa. Aqueous solutions with different concentrations (1 g L−1 inorganic salt solutions, 0.1 g L−1 dye solutions, and 0.2 g L−1 PEG solutions) were used to evaluate the performance of the NF membranes. Water flux (J) was calculated as follows:
(1)
J=
V
At
Solute rejection (R) was obtained by Eq. (2):
(2)
R= ( 1 -
C p
C f
) × 100 %
where V is the volume (L) of permeate water; A is the effective membrane area (m2); Cf and Cp are feed and permeate concentrations (g L−1), respectively. Electrical conductivity (Mettler Toledo FE-30) and UV–vis spectrometer (UV 2600) were used to measure the concentrations of inorganic salts and organic dyes, respectively. The concentrations of PEG were determined by total organic carbon analyzer (TOC, Shimadzu). The molecular weight of PEG with a rejection of 90% was regarded as the molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of the NF membranes.

The selective factor was calculated through Eq. (3)
(3)
α
salt / d y e
=
100 -
R
salt
100 -
R
dye
The antifouling performances were assessed by MYB and BSA. Specifically, the NF membrane was filtrated with DI water at 25 °C, 0.6 MPa for 4 h, recording the initial water flux as J0. Then, 100 ppm foulant aqueous solution was used as feed solution, measuring the corresponding water flux. After testing for 5 h, the fouled NF membrane was washed with DI water for 3 h, recording the water flux J1 again, and the flux recovery ratio was used to estimate the antifouling performance of NF membrane:
(4)
Flux recovery ratio=
J 1
J 0
× 100 %
The photocatalytic ability of the resultant NF membrane was evaluated by the degradation of MYB under visible light. The NF membrane was placed in the filtration cell and filtrated with MYB aqueous solution at 0.6 MPa. The water flux and MYB rejections were measured. Then, the filtrated NF membrane was removed from the filtration cell and put in a beaker replenished with 100 mL DI water facing up to the visible light. Condensate water was used to maintain the temperature of DI water in the beaker (25 °C). The visible light was generated by a 300 W xenon lamp (PLS-SXE-300 W, Beijing Perfect Light Technology Co., Ltd.). A 420 nm cut-off filter was furnished to ensure visible light irradiation. After visible light irradiation, the NF membrane was reinstalled to filtration cell to conduct NF test, recording the water flux and MYB rejections. The procedures were repeated three times. Besides, the change of dye amount absorbed on the membrane surface was adopted as an index to assess the photocatalytic self-cleaning performance (optical images).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterizations of CQD/TiO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a presented the FTIR spectra of CQDs, TiO2, and CQD/TiO2 NPs. In detail, the peak at 1635 cm−1 was attributed to the absorbed water in TiO2 and the broad absorption band below 1000 cm−1 belonged to TiO2 characteristic absorption [38,39]. The peak around 450–500 cm−1 was generally attributed to Ti-O [25]. Meanwhile, new peaks appeared in the spectrum of CQD/TiO2 NPs, the new peaks at 1565 cm−1 and 1395 cm−1 were ascribed to C-O in CQDs [40,41]. The FTIR results evidently demonstrated the incorporation of CQDs in CQD/TiO2 NPs. The XRD spectrum of CQDs showed a broad feature centered at 29.8°, which corresponded to the disordered graphitic-like species of CQDs [40]. The low signal-to-noise ratio was consistent with a significant portion of the CQDs being amorphous [40]. CQD/TiO2 NPs exhibited the characteristic diffraction peaks of anatase and rutile phases belonging to TiO2 (Fig. 2b) [42]. No obvious new peaks were observed, probably due to the low CQDs content in the CQD/TiO2 NPs and the low crystallinity of CQDs [42]. HRTEM was carried out to further characterize CQD/TiO2 NPs. CQDs with a number-average size of 5.1 ± 1.6 nm have been successfully synthesized (Fig. S1) and were used to decorate commercial TiO2 which contained both anatase and rutile phases. It revealed that several CQDs attach to the TiO2 surface (Fig. 2c). As shown in Fig. 2d, the fringe spacing of 0.32 nm and 0.35 nm belonged to the (110) plane of rutile TiO2, and (101) plane of anatase TiO2, respectively, while the interplanar spacing of 0.24 nm corresponded to the (100) plane of graphene carbon [27,40,43]. These results substantiated the successful preparation of CQD/TiO2 NPs.

UV–vis spectra of CQDs and CQD/TiO2 NPs serve as extra proof to confirm the formation of CQD/TiO2 NPs (Fig. 3 ). The CQDs exhibited a strong absorption around 266 nm, whereas, the absorption intensity of CQDs was quenched in the spectrum of CQD/TiO2 NPs. Besides, the incorporation of CQDs enhanced the absorption intensity of CQD/TiO2 NPs in the visible region, and the intensity increased with the increasing CQDs amount (Fig. S2) due to up-converted photoluminescence features of CQDs [42]. The adsorption edge was red-shifted compared with pure TiO2, which might be caused by the occupation of the interstitial sites of TiO2 lattice by carbon atoms, which narrowed the bandgap [42]. More importantly, almost no fluorescence emission of CQD/TiO2 NPs (not shown) suggested that recombination of photogenerated charge carriers in TiO2 was significantly retarded by the incorporation of CQDs [25]. These indicated that the CQD/TiO2 NPs can be excited and have improved visible light-driven photocatalytic activity. In this study, high-performing commercialized TiO2 was adopted to prepare CQD/TiO2 NPs. This TiO2 had a certain visible light catalytic ability probably due to dye sensitization, thus, ~ 17% of MYB were degraded, which phenomenon had been reported previously [44]. However, compared with TiO2, the higher visible light degradation efficiency of CQD/TiO2 NPs was exhibited (49.8%), indicating that the incorporation of CQDs indeed endowed CQD/TiO2 NPs with improved photocatalytic performance rather than dye sensitization (Fig. 3b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterizations of photocatalytic self-cleaning NF membranes

ce:section.ce:para: The chemical structure and surface composition were analyzed by ATR-FTIR and XPS to confirm the formation of polyamide selective layer. In Fig. 4 , compared with the spectrum of PSF substrate, the typical peak ascribed to CO of amide groups was observed in the spectra of PA-(1–5) at 1628 cm−1 [32], strongly demonstrating the formation of a polyamide selective layer via interfacial polymerization. No characteristic peaks of CQD/TiO2 NPs were shown due to the low additive amount. Therefore, XPS was adopted to further investigate the surface compositions. The atomic concentrations of C, N, O, and Ti elements of prepared membranes were presented in Table 1 . Both membranes possessed C, N, O elements, originating from the formed polyamide selective layers. The PA-4 membrane achieved higher O content, this might be attributed to the incorporation of CQD/TiO2 NPs [45]. Moreover, the Ti elements were only detected in PA-4 by XPS. Furthermore, EDS mapping of Ti element of PA-4 was also conducted to confirm the existence of CQD/TiO2 NPs in the NF membrane matrix (Fig. 5 ). The mapping image of the Ti element revealed good distribution in PA-4, indicating CQD/TiO2 NPs were successfully embedded in the polyamide NF matrix. This distribution seems quite homogenous without aggregations [46,47]. TiO2 NPs and CQD/TiO2 NPs aqueous solutions with the same concentration (0.035 wt%) were prepared. As shown in Fig. 5b, aqueous dispersion of TiO2 NPs easily aggregated in 20 min. In contrast, CQD/TiO2 NPs exhibited a better dispersion state and the incorporation of CQD alleviated the aggregation phenomenon. Some degree of precipitation occurred in CQD/TiO2 NPs aqueous solution at 30 mins, this might be ascribed to the relatively low content of CQDs on CQD/TiO2 NPs. Considering the immersion time of the aqueous phase was only 1 min, we believed that good dispersion of CQD/TiO2 NPs can ensure that no serious aggregation happened during interfacial polymerization. The above results verified that the CQD/TiO2 NPs have been successfully incorporated in the polyamide NF membrane matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NF performance of photocatalytic self-cleaning membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of CQD/TiO2 NPs contents on the NF membrane separation performances was presented in Fig. 8 . PA-1 had a water flux of 37.1 L m−2h−1 and Na2SO4 rejection of 97.9% due to its dense separation layer and negatively charged surface. The water flux consistently increased when the loading of CQD/TiO2 NPs increased to 0.035 wt%. Notably, the water flux of PA-4 was 67.22 L m−2h−1, which increased about 1.8 times, meanwhile the salt rejection of Na2SO4 was 96.7%. The trend of water flux was consistent with the variations of NF membrane surface morphology and hydrophilicity (Fig. 8b) because the hydrophilic and rougher surface will contribute to an improved water flux [49]. When further increased the concentration of CQD/TiO2 NPs, the water flux decreased to 55.54 L m−2h−1 accompanied by decreased salt rejection, which probably ascribed to the uneven distribution of CQD/TiO2 NPs in the polymer matrix [50]. Overall, the CQD/TiO2–mediated NF membrane with 0.035 wt% CQD/TiO2 NPs (PA-4) achieved a high water flux and retained Na2SO4 rejection of 96.7%, unveiling an excellent NF separation performance.

The separation performances of four inorganic salts were shown in Fig. 9 a. The salt rejections of PA-4 decreased in the sequence: Na2SO4 > MgSO4 > MgCl2 > NaCl. The decreased salt rejection sequence coincided with the negatively charged NF membrane (Fig. 9b) [51]. The repulsive forces between negatively charged NF surface and multivalence anions (SO4 2-) were stronger than that of monovalent anions (Cl-), thereby facilitating high rejections to Na2SO4 and MgSO4 while a relatively low rejection to MgCl2. The hydrated radius of Mg2+ (4.28 Å) was bigger than the radius of Na+(3.32 Å) [52], yielding higher rejection to MgCl2 than that of NaCl. This result vividly suggested that the Donnan effect and size sieving contribute to the separation behavior of PA-4. Notably, PA-4 showed similar rejections to Na2SO4 and MgSO4, whereas, a marked reduction in the rejections of MgCl2 and NaCl compared to those of PA-1. This phenomenon was might ascribe to the relatively looser separation layer and more negative surface charge after incorporation of CQD/TiO2 NPs. Na2SO4 and MgSO4 contained multivalent anion and larger hydrated radii, thus contributing to high rejections. Moreover, the rejections of PA-4 for four types of dyes were also shown in Fig. 9a. The rejections for negative CR and MYB of PA-4 remained high (99.4% and 99.1%, respectively), the selectivity factor of NaCl and MYB (
α
NaCl
MYB
) increased from 68.22 to 90.92, indicating improved selective separation performance. The high rejections to negatively charged dyes were contributed to the synergistic effect of the Donnan effect and steric hindrance. The negative charge of the membrane surface could effectively enhance the rejections of dyes with the same electrical properties owing to the Donnan effect. Besides, negatively charged dyes (CR = 696 Da and MYB = 799 Da) had high molecular weights, which was higher than the MWCO of PA-1, so the rejections of negatively charged dyes were also caused by steric hindrance. The rejections of PA-4 followed the sequence: CR > MYB. It was not fully consistent with the molecular weight of dyes and MWCO of PA-4, because the actual size of the dye was co-determined by the diffusivity and hydrophobic interactions of benzene rings of dyes [53]. CR molecules tended to aggregate in water and the larger molecular size resulted in higher rejection [54]. For positively charged dyes VBB (506 Da) and MB (319 Da), the rejection of VBB (98.9%) was much higher than that of MB (66.3%), which is ascribed to the large molecular size of VBB. This result provides further proof for the steric hindrance mechanism. Besides, because of the positively charged and low molecular weight, MB dyes were easily absorbed to the negatively charged membrane surface, resulting in the elimination of the Donnan effect and severe pore blockage. Thus, the rejection of MB was lowest in the four types of dyes. Fig. S3 indicated that surface absorption occurred for the positively charged MB dye.

The long-term NF separation performance of PA-1 and PA-4 to 1 g L−1 Na2SO4 solution at 25 °C, 0.6 MPa was showed in Fig. 9d. The water flux and Na2SO4 rejections of PA-1 and PA-4 remained stable during the continuous process, manifesting the good stability of the PA-4 NF membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifouling performance of CQD/TiO2–mediated NF membrane
ce:section.ce:para: MYB and BSA were chosen as model foulants to evaluate the antifouling performance of PA-4. As exhibited in Fig. 10 a, when filtrated MYB solution, the water flux of PA-1 sharply declined due to adsorption/deposition of MYB dyes on the surface, and the fouled MYB on membrane surface can partially be removed by DI water washing. After 2.5 cycles of experiments, the flux recovery ratio of PA-1 was 82%. Compared with PA-1, the passive antifouling ability of PA-4 was enhanced with the incorporation of CQD/TiO2 NPs, showing higher flux recovery ratios (89.2%). Similar behaviors of PA-1 and PA-4 were found in dealing with BSA aqueous solution, and the flux recovery ratios of PA-4 and PA-1 were 86% and 80% respectively (Fig. 10b). These results manifested that the incorporation of CQD/TiO2 NPs improved the antifouling performance because the higher hydrophilicity of the membrane surface could significantly prevent the membrane surface from adsorbing hydrophobic organic foulants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-cleaning and stability study of CQD/TiO2–mediated NF membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The incorporation of CQD/TiO2 NPs can reduce the adsorption of dyes on membrane surfaces to a certain extent (Fig. 11 ). Simultaneously, due to the photocatalytic capacity of CQD/TiO2 NPs, the self-cleaning performance of the resultant PA-4 membrane was evaluated. The cyclic fouling-visible light irradiation experiments were conducted. Specifically, 100 ppm MYB solution was filtrated by PA-4 for 5 h, then taken out the membrane and placed under visible light for 1 h, the NF performances were tested again with 100 ppm MYB solution. The procedures were repeated three times.

During membrane filtration, MYB can be absorbed on membrane surface via hydrophobic interactions, partial MYB dyes could be removed by physical DI water washing (i. e., reversible fouling), whereas, irreversible fouling existed concurrently. Thus, as shown in Fig. 11, the flux recovery ratios of PA-4 by DI water washing in three cycles were 92.9%, 89.2%, and 86.6%, respectively, furthermore, the extent of irreversible fouling was more serious with prolonging the fouling cycles. Even though the antifouling performance was improved by the incorporation of CQD/TiO2 NPs, it was still inefficient to retrieve water flux. Compared with physical water washing, the flux recovery ratios were significantly improved by exposing PA-4 to visible light for 1 h. The flux recovery ratios in the first, second, and third cycles were 98.5%, 98.3%, and 97.9% respectively. The functions of CQD included the electron reservoirs with reduced electron-hole recombination and the photosensitizers to enhance the harvest of visible light, thus, CQD/TiO2 NPs were activated by visible light irradiation, and photo-electrons and holes were generated, the photoinduced electrons were trapped by O2 to form O
2 −, and oxidize OH− and H2O to OH
by photoinduced holes [42]. The absorbed MYB dyes were degraded after visible-light irradiation for 1 h benefiting from the photocatalytic ability of CQD/TiO2 NPs, therefore, the water flux could recover to a higher level. After three cyclic fouling-irradiation experiments, the water flux was merely decreased from 56.94 L m−2h−1 to 55.80 L m−2h−1. The mechanism for visible light-driven photocatalytic activity of CQD/TiO2 NPs has been reported in the literature [38,42,55,56]. The successful applications of CQD/TiO2 NPs in the degradation of gaseous acetaldehyde and N-benzylideneaniline, water splitting, and nitric oxide removal [38,42,55,56], can provide proof for the exclusion of the dye sensitization mechanism.

More importantly, exhibiting good self-cleaning ability and retaining performance stability (reusability) is a critical challenge for the applications of photocatalytic NF membranes because it affects the lifespan. As shown in Fig. 11, a high MYB rejection (blue dot) was exhibited by PA-4 during the cyclic fouling-visible light irradiation tests, validating excellent stability, i.e., the PA-4 NF membrane structure was maintained under photocatalytic degradation. This was ascribed to the protective effect of polydopamine coating [35]. Polydopamine has been verified to have prominent free radical scavenging ability, the coating can protect NF membrane from being damaged, remaining the intact NF membrane structure after repeated visible light irradiation [36]. These results demonstrated that the prepared PA-4 achieved good stability and self-cleaning performance under visible light irradiation.

Further, the continuous filtration experiment of MYB solution was conducted on PA-1 and PA-4 for strengthening membrane fouling extent (i.e., for increasing MYB absorption amount on membrane surface). After continuous fouling PA-1 and PA-4 with 100 ppm MYB for 72 h, the fouled membranes were irradiated under visible irradiation for 1 h. As shown in Fig. 12 a, even though the adsorbed MYB molecules were slightly degraded due to their self-sensitization property [57], the color was still apparent on the PA-1 NF membrane surface under irradiation. For PA-4, the blue color caused by MYB adsorption was significantly disappeared after irradiation. The disappearance of MYB on PA-4 under visible light-irradiation mainly resulted from the successful introduction of CQD/TiO2 NPs. As shown in Fig. 12 b-c, the water flux of PA-1 decreased about 23.4% after visible light irradiation due to the lack of efficient photocatalytic ability, whereas, the declination of PA-4 was only about 6.49% with stable MYB rejection. These results substantiated that incorporation of CQD/TiO2 NPs endowed polyamide NF membrane with visible light-driven self-cleaning ability, thus, the water flux of prepared NF membrane can be recovered by visible light irradiation without sacrifice of dye rejections.

Indeed, due to the opaque filtration apparatus, PA-4 needed to remove from the filtration cell for visible light exposure and failed to achieve the in situ self-cleaning ability (where the membrane is never removed from the filtration apparatus). More effective work should be conducted to design new membrane modules for the applications of visible light-driven self-cleaning NF membranes in industry [11].

Visible light-driven photocatalytic CQD/TiO2 NPs were successfully fabricated, and self-cleaning NF membranes were constructed by incorporation of photocatalytic CQD/TiO2 NPs during interfacial polymerization. The incorporation of CQD/TiO2 NPs endowed the resulting NF membranes with improved water flux and remarkable dye removal ability. Under the optimized preparation conditions, the water flux of PA-4 was significantly enhanced to 67.22 L m−2h−1, which increased about 1.8 times, and the salt rejection of Na2SO4 was 96.7%. Meanwhile, the excellent dye rejections were maintained (CR, 99.8%; MYB, 99.1%). More importantly, the dye degradation capacity was achieved due to the unique attributes of CQD/TiO2 NPs, which allowed the PA-4 membranes to develop a self-cleaning ability. In specific, the MYB dyes absorbed on the PA-4 membrane surface can be facilely removed under visible light irradiation, the water flux can recover close to the original flux without sacrifice of the dye rejections, demonstrating excellent self-cleaning performance and stability during cycles fouling- irradiation tests. This work offers an avenue for visible light-driven NF membrane for alieving membrane fouling and sustainable separations.

Yifang Mi: Methodology, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Na Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation. Xingyu Fang: Investigation, Validation. Jia Cao: Investigation, Validation. Meng Tao: Investigation, Validation. Zhihai Cao: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21908200 and 51573168), the Talent Scientific Research Fund of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University (No. 18012218-Y), the Key Research & Development Program of Zhejiang Province (2021C03196), and National Undergraduate Training Programs for Innovation and Entrepreneurship of China (201910338041).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2021.119500.

ce:section.ce:para:
